title: Whittled sycamore letter opener
subtitle: A first seasoned-wood project from a flat blank
excerpt: A letter opener is one of the most useful small whittling projects for a beginner: a symmetrical flat blade shape with a simple taper, no hollowing required, and a finished piece that is actually used. Seasoned sycamore is the starting material here for stability and the clean, pale surface it gives the finished edge.
sourceNotes: Letter-opener and paper-knife forms documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885). General whittling instruction from Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898).

content.text: A letter opener makes a good first project in seasoned wood. The shape is forgiving, the tools are few, and the finished piece is something a person actually uses. The form is a long, flat taper with a blunt point. It is wide at the handle and narrows to a thin, smooth blade edge. There is no hollowing, no complex joinery, and no tight radius to navigate. Sycamore is the recommended species. It carves cleanly with a knife even when seasoned, gives a very pale surface, and is widely sold as craft blanks from wood-carving suppliers.

, 22 cm long, 3.5 cm wide, 1.5 cm thick. Suppliers who sell carving blanks stock this kind of section; sycamore and lime are the most common choices for first-project flat blanks. Alternatively, cut a section from any kiln-dried sycamore board that meets these dimensions. The grain should run along the length of the blank, not across it. An end-grain section would split along the blade taper as it dried; a long-grain section is stable.

Lime (basswood) is the easiest alternative: it carves more readily than sycamore from a seasoned blank and is the preferred wood for European chip-carving, where its fine, even grain produces consistent results. Beech is harder and holds fine details well but requires a sharper edge and more effort. For a first project, sycamore or lime are the most forgiving.
A sloyd knife that will take a hair off the back of the forearm. A
charged with compound, close at hand. Sandpaper in 180, 240, and 320 grits for the finishing ladder. Raw linseed oil for the finish. A pencil for marking the profile.

A stable seat at a comfortable working height. Good light from the front or from a slight angle so the surface shows the knife marks clearly. The blank can be held in the hand throughout this project; no bench vise or clamp is needed. If the blank is very short, a piece of non-slip mat on a table gives a stable base for the moments when both hands are involved in positioning.

Pencil the outline of the letter opener on the blank. The handle occupies the first 8 cm, widening slightly toward the hand end. The blade runs from there to the tip, tapering from 3 cm wide at the handle-blade join to a rounded point at the far end. The blade is not sharpened to a working edge; a letter opener needs a smooth, blunt edge that does not cut fingers but slides cleanly under a paper flap.

along the sides of the blank, remove material from the edges to bring the outline close to the pencilled line. Work down both sides toward the tip. Take off small amounts at a time rather than trying to reach the final profile in a few large cuts. The grain in a seasoned sycamore blank will run cleanly if cut with the direction of the grain; check the cut surface for roughness and reverse direction if needed.

The blade section (the forward 14 cm) needs to be thinned toward the edge. Hold the blank on its edge and take push cuts along the flat face, angling from the full thickness at the spine toward a thinner edge. The target is around 3 mm at the edge, tapering from 12 to 15 mm at the spine. Both faces should be worked equally to keep the letter opener symmetrical. Work slowly in this section; the wood is thinner and a misplaced cut can go too deep. Check the thickness by holding the piece up to a light and looking for even translucency across the blade.

The handle can be left flat or carved into an oval section for a better hand feel. Oval cross-section: take push cuts from each corner of the rectangular handle toward the centre of the face, working at a 45-degree angle to the face. This removes the four sharp corners and produces eight facets. A further round of lighter cuts blends the facets to an oval. The thumb-pivot cut works well here for the short, precise cuts needed to blend the transitions.

Sand through the grit sequence: 180, 240, 320. Wrap each sheet around a small block or fold it to a firm pad. Sand along the grain, not across it. 180 grit removes knife marks; 240 smooths the surface; 320 prepares for the oil. After each grit, wipe away the dust with a dry cloth before moving to the next. The finished surface at 320 should be smooth to the touch with no visible scratches.

Apply raw linseed oil with a cloth, working it into the grain. Allow to penetrate for one hour, then wipe off every trace of surface oil with a clean dry cloth. The surface should look barely changed. Leave to cure for two weeks. Raw linseed oil on a letter opener is a light protective finish against the oils from hands, not a moisture-barrier film, so re-application every six months or whenever the wood feels dry is sensible.
Add a kolrosing border around the handle. Fine incised lines rubbed with ground coffee give the handle a decorative pattern without tools beyond the same knife. Apply before the first oil coat.

Cut the blank longer (28 cm) for a full-size paper knife. The proportions stay the same; the blade simply extends. A longer blade needs thinning over a greater length, which requires more care at the unsupported tip.

Cherry as an alternative species. Cherry has a warm reddish tone that deepens with age and handling, produces a very clean knife surface, and takes a fine sanded finish. It is harder than sycamore and needs a sharper edge to cut cleanly.

Wipe with a dry cloth after use. Do not leave in a damp or very humid place; the wood is sealed with oil rather than a film finish and will respond to extremes of moisture. Re-oil once or twice a year by wiping on a small amount of raw linseed oil, allowing it to soak in for an hour, and wiping off the surplus.

attrs.intro: What can go wrong with this project.

items.fix: Hold the piece up to the light and check symmetry by eye. Take light passes on the thicker face until both faces appear even. Work slowly near the final dimensions.
items.cause: Unequal amounts of wood were removed from each face during thinning.
items.symptom: The blade section is thicker on one face than the other

items.fix: Round the break to a new tip shape that is slightly shorter. For future work, stop cuts at the tip before approaching it with push cuts will prevent the grain from running off the end.
items.cause: A push cut ran with the grain past the intended stopping point at the tip, or the tip was reached from the wrong direction.
items.symptom: The tip broke off during shaping

items.fix: Take a few more rounds of push cuts across the corners of the handle to chamfer them further. A comfortable handle is oval or round in cross-section, not square.
items.cause: The cross-section is too rectangular with sharp edges.
items.symptom: The handle is difficult to hold comfortably

attrs.heading: Common problems
